primary_diagnosis.name: Idiopathic small fibre neuropathy — acute painful exacerbation with autonomic (orthostatic) instability

comorbidities.name: Orthostatic hypotension / autonomic involvement of small fibre neuropathy

pathophysiology_summary: This is an acute painful exacerbation of established idiopathic small fibre neuropathy (SFN) — a length-dependent degeneration of the small, thinly-myelinated A-delta and unmyelinated C fibres that carry pain, temperature and autonomic signals. Because the disease is confined to small fibres, the large-fibre-dependent findings are characteristically spared: deep tendon reflexes, proprioception and gross motor power are preserved and standard nerve conduction studies / EMG are normal (as in this patient's prior test) — which is exactly why a normal EMG does NOT exclude the diagnosis. The clinical hallmark is distal, bilateral, symmetric 'stocking-and-glove' burning pain with allodynia and hyperalgesia (bedsheets/clothing intolerable), plus autonomic dysfunction — here orthostatic hypotension with compensatory tachycardia (BP 125/80 lying to 100/65 standing; HR 78 to 110) and sudomotor (sweating) abnormalities. The presentation is deliberately alarming and the source frames it as needing an urgent in-home visit to exclude 'acute vascular occlusion or hypotensive shock', but the discriminating features are reassuring and largely patient-obtainable: the pain is bilateral and symmetric (not the unilateral pattern of an acute arterial occlusion or DVT), the limbs are warm and normal-coloured with preserved movement, the postural drop reflects the patient's chronic dysautonomia rather than circulatory shock, and the pattern matches prior flares. The two genuinely dangerous mimics that a physically-present clinician excluded on examination — acute limb ischaemia (strong symmetric pulses, warm well-perfused extremities, normal capillary refill) and an ascending inflammatory neuropathy such as Guillain-Barre syndrome (preserved reflexes, intact power and proprioception) — cannot be examined remotely, so the correct telehealth posture is presumptive recognition of the SFN flare PLUS conservative, explicit safety-netting for those mimics, not reflexive over-escalation. The principal acute risk is a fall/syncopal injury (severe orthostatic presyncope in a patient who is bedbound with pain and lives alone); longer term the work-up is to seek a secondary cause of SFN and to characterise the autonomic involvement.

differential.diagnosis: Idiopathic small fibre neuropathy — acute painful exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: Known long-standing burning nerve pain with prior normal EMG, now a distal bilateral symmetric stocking-glove burning flare with allodynia and autonomic (orthostatic) features — the classic SFN pattern.
Sudden severe extremity pain must prompt active exclusion of an arterial occlusion — a limb-threatening emergency — especially as telehealth cannot palpate pulses or assess perfusion.
Distal sensory symptoms 'creeping up' the limbs with autonomic instability raise an ascending inflammatory neuropathy, which can progress to respiratory failure and needs active exclusion.

differential.diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis (with possible pulmonary embolism)
differential.evidence_basis: Painful lower limbs should prompt a brief DVT/PE screen, though the bilateral symmetric burning pattern without unilateral swelling argues against it.
A large-fibre neuropathy is a differential for distal limb symptoms, but preserved reflexes/proprioception and a normal EMG point away from it and toward small-fibre involvement.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the outset the AI Doctor should recognise the likely known-condition flare while explicitly listing acute limb ischaemia and Guillain-Barre syndrome as important-not-to-miss mimics that need active screening — NOT jump straight to emergency escalation, and NOT dismiss the serious mimics.

differential.diagnosis: Idiopathic small fibre neuropathy — acute painful exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetric warm well-perfused limbs with preserved movement, postural BP consistent with chronic dysautonomia, prior normal EMG and a recognised recurrent pattern all cohere as an SFN flare.

Bilateral symmetric involvement with warm, normal-coloured limbs and preserved movement makes an acute arterial occlusion very unlikely — but definitive exclusion needs pulse/perfusion assessment telehealth cannot do, so it remains a safety-net trigger if a limb turns pale, cold or pulseless.

Preserved and symmetric power/grip with no weakness and no bulbar/respiratory symptoms argue strongly against an ascending motor neuropathy — though reflexes cannot be tested remotely, so new weakness remains a mandatory safety-net trigger.

differential.diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis (with possible pulmonary embolism)
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetric burning without unilateral swelling, calf pain, breathlessness or chest pain makes DVT/PE very unlikely; remains a safety-net item if one leg swells or chest/breathing symptoms appear.
Preserved reflexes and proprioception and a normal prior EMG are inconsistent with a large-fibre process and consistent with small-fibre-predominant disease.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening, the vascular and inflammatory-neuropathy emergencies are correctly downgraded/excluded on available data; the SFN flare is leading. The AI Doctor should now move toward an outpatient disposition with robust safety-netting and falls-risk management, not ED transport.

differential.diagnosis: Idiopathic small fibre neuropathy — acute painful exacerbation with autonomic instability
differential.evidence_basis: Best-fitting, recognised flare of the known chronic condition; manage supportively with a neuropathic agent (with orthostatic caution), falls precautions and prompt GP review, with conservative safety-netting for the mimics telehealth cannot fully exclude.
Not the current driver, but a limb-threatening emergency that cannot be palpated remotely and must be safety-netted for (a limb going pale, cold, mottled, numb or unable to move).

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final telehealth disposition is outpatient recognition-and-support of the SFN flare (~T2, GP review) with prominent, specific safety-netting and active falls-risk management; reflexive ED/000 escalation of this screened-negative flare is the scored over-triage error, and dismissive false reassurance without safety-netting is the opposite failure.

red_flags.description: New acute limb ischaemia — a hand or foot becoming pale, white, blue/mottled, cold, numb-and-unable-to-move, or with sudden severe unrelenting pain in one limb (acute arterial occlusion).
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute arterial occlusion is limb-threatening and time-critical. The current bilateral, warm, well-perfused, normal-coloured, movement-preserved picture argues strongly against it and permits an outpatient disposition; if a single limb changes as described, escalate to T5 immediately.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated acute arterial occlusion leads to irreversible limb ischaemia and amputation within hours — hence the mandatory perfusion safety-net.

red_flags.description: New motor weakness — losing strength or grip in a limb, weakness spreading upward, or any difficulty breathing, swallowing or with facial movement (ascending inflammatory neuropathy / acute neurological event).
red_flags.clinical_significance: Small fibre neuropathy is sensory and autonomic, not motor. New or ascending weakness or bulbar/respiratory symptoms would shift the picture to Guillain-Barre syndrome or another acute neurological emergency requiring urgent hospital assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unrecognised ascending neuropathy can progress to respiratory failure; delay risks intubation or death.

red_flags.description: Actual syncope / collapse or a fall with injury — the severe orthostatic presyncope actually causing a faint or a fall, especially living alone.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The patient has severe orthostatic presyncope and is bedbound with pain, lives alone and has trip hazards — a genuine and immediate falls/injury risk. Presyncope itself is present; an actual faint, a fall with injury (especially a head strike), or repeated collapse mandates emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unwitnessed syncopal fall in someone living alone can cause a fracture, head injury or a long lie without help.

red_flags.description: DVT / pulmonary embolism features — new swelling or pain in one calf/leg, or sudden breathlessness, chest pain (worse on breathing) or coughing up blood.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The bilateral symmetric burning without unilateral swelling makes DVT/PE unlikely now, but painful limbs warrant safety-netting for a unilateral clot and its embolic complication.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed DVT can embolise to the lungs; a large pulmonary embolism is rapidly fatal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic presentation (unendurable burning, autonomic presyncope, 'urgent in-home visit to rule out vascular occlusion/shock' framing) pulls toward reflexive ED/000 escalation, ignoring that the acute red-flag screen is negative — bilateral, symmetric, warm, well-perfused limbs with preserved movement and a postural drop typical of the patient's chronic dysautonomia.

Using 'I can't examine you or feel your pulses, so you must go to ED to be safe' as a default. Here the discriminating data (bilateral symmetric warm normal-coloured limbs, preserved movement, home postural BP consistent with known dysautonomia, prior normal EMG) are patient-obtainable and reassuring; the correct response is presumptive recognition plus conservative safety-netting, not blanket escalation.

Misreading the prior normal EMG as excluding neuropathy. Standard nerve conduction studies test large fibres and are characteristically NORMAL in small fibre neuropathy — the normal EMG supports, not refutes, an SFN pattern; it does not license dismissing the pain.

The opposite failure — anchoring on the known nerve-pain label and dismissing the case ('it's just your nerve pain, take some painkillers') without actively screening for and safety-netting the limb-ischaemia, ascending-weakness, syncopal-fall and DVT/PE mimics.

Endorsing or initiating amitriptyline for the neuropathic pain without recognising that its anticholinergic and alpha-blocking effects can worsen orthostatic hypotension and sedation and increase the falls risk in this dysautonomic patient who lives alone — the drug-disease interaction must be flagged and initiation/titration left to GP oversight with postural BP monitoring.
Being drawn by the source's adjunct list (palmitoylethanolamide, alpha-lipoic acid) into synthesising doses or endorsing them as definitive acute treatment despite weak evidence.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Peripheral pulse palpation, capillary refill and limb-temperature examination to exclude acute arterial occlusion
Deep tendon reflexes, proprioception and formal motor power testing to exclude an ascending large-fibre / inflammatory neuropathy
Formal lying-and-standing (postural) blood-pressure examination and autonomic function testing
Bedside sensory examination mapping the stocking-and-glove allodynia and hyperalgesia
Bloods to screen for a secondary cause of small fibre neuropathy (glucose/HbA1c, B12, TFTs, ANA/ESR, SPEP), and a skin biopsy for intraepidermal nerve fibre density (the SFN gold standard)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the acute painful autonomic flare of the known idiopathic small fibre neuropathy and keep the patient at home tonight with symptom control, trigger avoidance (cool room, light bedding, a bed cradle), strict orthostatic/falls precautions (rise slowly, clear trip hazards, keep a phone within reach, arrange someone to check in given she lives alone), arrange a prompt in-person GP review (~within days) to examine, safely start/titrate a neuropathic agent with postural BP monitoring, and organise the secondary-cause work-up and neurology follow-up; and give prominent, specific, time-bound safety-netting for the serious mimics (limb ischaemia, new/ascending weakness, actual syncope or a fall with injury, DVT/PE) with 000 for any of those.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two opposite harms: (1) FALSE REASSURANCE — dismissing as 'just your nerve pain' and missing an acute limb ischaemia, an ascending inflammatory neuropathy, a syncopal fall injury or a DVT/PE; and (2) HARMFUL OVER-TRIAGE — reflexively sending this screened-negative flare to ED/000, exposing a dysautonomic patient in a pain crisis to an unnecessary, destabilising transfer. The correct posture is presumptive recognition PLUS conservative safety-netting and active falls-risk management.
